title: Housing's 30-year super cycle is over
full_text:
The immediate feedback Chanticleer received from the investment community on Tuesday night was visceral.
"Muppet show," one big fund manager texted soon after Treasurer Jim Chalmers got to his feet in Parliament to deliver his budget speech.
The budget's central measures - the biggest set of changes to wealth in decades, including changes to capital gains tax, negative gearing, and the taxation of trusts - may have been well flagged, but they provoked anger among many professional money managers.
Where Chalmers sees a levelling of the intergenerational playing field, many investors see a blow to aspiration; the grandfathering of CGT and negative gearing means older generations lock in many of the tax advantages they've long benefited from, while younger Australians are constrained by a new, less generous set of rules.
Investors had also been hoping against hope that these new tax measures would be offset by real spending restraint and a clear plan to boost productivity and grow the economy. Instead, savings were mainly spent, with the government largely relying on big cuts to the NDIS that are still a few years away, and windfall revenue gains to deliver a tiny improvement in the budget bottom line over the next five years.
The investment community is now furiously trying to work through the budget's implications. Inevitably, their analysis has quickly focused on the only topic that ever seems to matter in Australia: housing.
A 10.5 per cent plunge in the share price of Commonwealth Bank, the country's biggest mortgage lender, on Wednesday, only reinforced that message.
UBS strategist Richard Schellbach says the changes to CGT and negative gearing remove the advantage property had as an investment class and improve "the relative attractiveness of investments in other asset classes, which should see stronger flows towards equities, funds and platforms".
But there are wrinkles within that. As Goldman Sachs strategist Matthew Ross has noted, the attractiveness of growth stocks is dulled by what could be a doubling of capital gains tax for high income earners in the top marginal tax bracket, who could now end up paying a world-record 40 per cent on capital gains.
On the other hand, high-yielding dividend stocks become relatively more attractive, with one exception - the banks.
Morgan Stanley bank analyst Richard Wiles argues that any increase in the attraction of the big banks as yield plays will be dulled by the impending hit to housing market sentiment, as property investors digest the twin blows of Reserve Bank rate increases and the budget's tax changes.
For Wiles, this is more than a temporary hiccup that the housing market can quickly adapt to. Rather, this is the end of an era.
"In our view, favourable tax treatment is one of the reasons why there has been a 30-year housing 'super-cycle' in Australia. However, changes to property-related tax concessions could have a profound effect on the long-term demand for investment properties. All else equal, less leverage and less property price appreciation imply more modest mortgage growth," Wiles says.
The government won't say it so directly, but the end of that super cycle is exactly what it intends here. While the tax take from the CGT and negative gearing tax changes will build over time given they have been grandfathered - the financial impact over the next five years is a relatively modest $3.7 billion - what matters today is the message that Chalmers and his housing minister Clare O'Neil want to send: investing in housing is no longer the golden ticket to wealth that it has been for 30 years.
Some might call that gesture politics, but shifting the psyche of Australians when it comes to property ownership and investment does matter. I can tell you that with two teenagers at home, both of whom regularly joke about how unlikely it is that they will ever be able to afford a home.
We all laugh. But it's not funny that a 14-year-old kid can so clearly see what he's up against.
Whether the CGT and negative gearing changes make a meaningful, long-term difference to housing supply is, of course, questionable; the government's own estimates that 75,000 first home buyers will now be able to enter the market are as paltry as they are rubbery.
But bank analysts are in no doubt as to the immediate impact on investors.
According to UBS analyst John Storey, investors currently account for about 20 per cent of total mortgages, and about 40 per cent of new mortgage flow. But Macquarie analyst Carlos Cacho says the tax changes in the budget reduce the borrowing power of a housing investor by between 10 per cent and 20 per cent. Add in the rise in official interest rates we've seen since the start of the year (Cacho is assuming we get one more hike to add to the three delivered) and the borrowing power of an investor is reduced by between 20 per cent and 30 per cent.
Clearly, that matters for bank earnings. Wiles estimates that mortgage credit growth could fall from the current annualised growth rate of 7.5 per cent to about 5.5 per cent in the 2027 financial year, with investor loan growth dropping from 10 per cent at present to about 7 per cent.
Barrenjoey chief economist Jo Masters was expecting house prices to fall 1.5 per cent in the 2027 financial year as rate increases took hold, but Tuesday night's tax changes have made her revise that to a 3.5 per cent fall, before first-home buyers step in to a still undersupplied market and push prices up 3.5 per cent.
Wiles isn't so sure the damage to the mortgage market will be so muted. He says that if the RBA were to maintain its tightening bias, if the economy were to slow sharply, if property investors were to pull back and if house prices were to fall 10 per cent, mortgage growth could slow to 3 per cent in 2027.
There are a lot of assumptions there, of course, but there is some historical precedent for this. In 2019, when investor loan growth stalled for most of the year, growth in the mortgage market slowed to 3 per cent. Cacho notes that bank stocks underperformed the market by 20 per cent during that period. "With bank share prices correlated with house prices/house price expectations, any slowdown in the housing market could see banks underperform," Cacho says.
CBA's March quarter trading update on Wednesday morning probably couldn't have arrived at a worse time. Although the numbers were softer than expected - cash profit of $2.7 billion was 2 per cent lower than the market was looking for - it was a loan impairment charge of $316 million that appeared to surprise investors, although the bulk of this was from CBA following peers and increasing its collective provisions to reflect the increased geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainty we are all staring into.
But with the spotlight already on CBA, it was no surprise that almost every analyst was quick to note that it has the largest investor loan book, and is thus arguably the most exposed to the slowdown in the coming property investor activity.
The 10.5 per cent fall in CBA's share price looks overdone in the context of much smaller declines across the rest of the banking sector, but as we've seen, big share price moves on even small signs of weakening revenue and earnings momentum are now par for the course on today's ASX.
Still, we shouldn't miss the bigger point here: in a matter of months, the overarching environment for the banks has changed, and quite rapidly, too. Tax changes are set to slow the growth of the most important product in Australian banking. Interest rate increases have to slow the economy. And the energy shock has forced the banks and the broader market to think about bad debts in a way they haven't needed to for a while.
This is a moment of real flux - for the Australian economy, as much as the banks.
JAMES THOMSON
j.thomson@afr.
summary: The Australian government's recent budget changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing are expected to end a 30-year housing investment boom, making property less attractive for investors and potentially lowering house prices.
author: James Thomson
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Treasurer Jim Chalmers announced major tax changes in the budget.
Investors are unhappy, seeing these changes as a setback for wealth building.
Commonwealth Bank's share price dropped 10.5% following the budget announcement.
Changes could reduce housing investment demand and borrowing power by 10-30%.
House prices might fall by 3.5% in 2027 before recovering.
ticker_line: End of housing investment boom signals major changes
